ops.title: Daemon conventions — detached spawn, trust, takeover, drain (daemonkit + non-Go reference impls)
ops.body:
daemonkit is the fleet's answer to one recurring problem: a CLI-adjacent daemon that spawns itself detached on first use, owns a unix socket single-entrant, gets replaced by a strictly newer build without dropping work, and trusts its peers by kernel-reported credentials plus code signature. The root contract is five primitives — detached spawn, single-entrant socket ownership, version-aware takeover, drain-on-upgrade, and codesign peer trust (doc.go:1-4). This doc records the conventions each package enforces, the launchd/SMAppService matrix, and two non-Go reference implementations that show which parts of the contract survive translation and which get deliberately dropped.

## Detached spawn and process identity (proc)

`proc.Spawn` starts the child in its own session (`Setsid: true`) with stdin nil, stdout/stderr appended to a 0600 log file, and an async reap goroutine — `Setsid` detaches the session, not the parent-child link, so `cmd.Wait` must still run or the exit strands a zombie (proc/spawn.go:136-156). Racing spawns are harmless because the child refuses to start if the socket is already owned; `Available`/`CanHost` gate every attempt and an optional `Gate` records the launch durably before it happens (proc/spawn.go:24-52).

The hard contract on the child side: a fork+exec child inherits every non-CLOEXEC parent descriptor, and pure Go has no fork hook to sweep them spawner-side. Every long-lived child binary calls `proc.CloseInheritedFDs` first in `main`, which closes every inherited fd ≥ 3 whose FD_CLOEXEC flag is unset — a leaked session-lease fd would otherwise stay pinned for the child's lifetime (proc/spawn.go:17-23, proc/closefds.go:11-19).

Single-entrant ownership is an exclusive flock on `<socket>.lock`, held for the listener's whole life. The lock file is NEVER unlinked: unlinking a held lock would let a third process flock a fresh inode and believe it won (proc/singleentrant.go:15-20). On contention, `Evict` is consulted at most once — `evicted=true` polls for the evictee's flock (released only at process exit, which can lag socket death), `evicted=false` refuses with `ErrPeerStarting` (proc/singleentrant.go:22-59).

Process identity is `{PID, StartTime, Comm, Generation}`, and the reaper revalidates all four against the live process table before it signals — a PID alone is never kill authority, because a reused PID gets a fresh start stamp (proc/reaper.go:22-38, proc/probe.go:3-8). Probe errors fail closed: `ErrNoProcess` is the only definitive "gone"; any other error means Undetermined and no action (proc/probe.go:23-27). On Darwin, `withChildNprocCap` counts the UID's live processes and temporarily lowers RLIMIT_NPROC to that count plus 400 headroom across the fork, so the child subtree inherits the cap and a runaway re-spawn loop starves at EAGAIN instead of fork-bombing the host (proc/rlimit_darwin.go:13-28). This is also why the repo's tests only run through `scripts/test.sh`.

## Wire protocol (wire)

The transport core is LF-delimited JSON: one object per line over a `net.Conn`, each read bounded by `MaxLine` (default 1 MiB via `DefaultMaxLine`), with `ErrFrameTooLarge` on overflow — the codec never silently truncates (wire/framing.go:18-23). Consumers override the cap where their payloads demand it; cc-interact runs 64 MiB via its `Config.MaxFrameBytes`. Peer credentials are read exactly once, at accept, via `PeerFromConn` (wire/peer.go:19), and per-op deadline ladders bound each read and write (wire/framing.go, wire/ladder.go).

The server checks trust before dispatch, on every request: the same-UID floor is unconditional and a `Trust` hook can only tighten it, never relax it (wire/server.go:16, wire/server.go:87-89, wire/server.go:403-410). The lifecycle op names — `health`, `shutdown`, `hello`, `handoff` — are reserved; registering one panics so a consumer handler can never shadow the daemon layer (wire/server.go:36-42).

The lifecycle protocol itself is frozen. `lifeproto` speaks a FLAT envelope — `{"v":1,"op":...}` plus op fields at the top level — and its field names, op strings, and key order are a compatibility contract with deployed peers, including the Swift binding. Both bindings generate from one schema and pin identical bytes via a shared golden fixture (wire/lifeproto/doc.go:1-12). Capability detection goes through Health feature flags, not version sniffing: `Health.HasFeature` is the authority for whether a peer supports an op like handoff (daemon/peer.go:13-17, daemon/peer.go:48).

## Peer trust (trust)

`trust.Policy` enforces a same-EUID floor on every platform; on a signed Darwin build a configured `Requirement` additionally validates a designated requirement against the peer's audit token (trust/policy.go:1-8). A valid `Requirement` pins BOTH the Team ID and the signing identifier: a TeamID-only requirement grants every binary on the team authority over every socket, and an identifier-only one is not globally unique across teams (trust/policy.go:27-31). A configured requirement with no available verifier — non-Darwin build, or no audit token — fails closed with `ErrNoVerifier`, never a silent downgrade to UID-only; UID-only trust exists solely in `daemonkit_unsigned` test builds, which release CI must reject (trust/policy.go:22-25). `AllowUnhardened` is the one bypass, for a consumer shipping an audited third-party dylib it cannot library-validate, and it never relaxes the designated requirement or the floor (trust/policy.go:37-43).

The documented limitation: `LOCAL_PEERTOKEN` resolves the peer socket's process at query time, not connect-frozen. A same-UID peer can fork a holder and exec a genuine signed binary to lend its identity, and the floor does not confine fd delegation across a setuid/SCM_RIGHTS boundary. This is a known-unsound binding for code authentication; a surface needing a real per-message identity guarantee uses XPC (`setCodeSigningRequirement`), not this Policy (trust/policy.go:91-97). The Swift mirror, `PeerTrust`, states the same floor, the same fail-closed requirement path, and the same XPC caveat (Sources/DaemonKit/PeerTrust.swift:8-26).

## Version ordering and takeover (version, daemon)

A version is either a `Release{Major,Minor,Patch}` triple or a `Dev{BuildUnixNano}` build. Every Dev outranks every Release — a dev daemon is never evicted by a release, and a dev binary always takes over a release daemon — and ties are never newer, so an equal version never triggers eviction (version/version.go:1-8, version/version.go:26-29). The dev sentinel is `9999.<unix-nanos>.0-dev`, which parses as a valid triple, so `Parse` classifies Dev before any triple parsing — otherwise newest-dev-wins inverts (version/version.go:70-74). The running binary's version is resolved and memoized on first call: an unstamped "dev" build stats its resolved executable exactly once for the mtime-derived DevString, so a rebuild on disk never changes a running daemon's reported version (version/version.go:144-149).

Takeover is successor-initiated and newer-wins. The eviction ladder for a `RequestDaemon` (owns no external resources): Health probe, then a Shutdown request, then a grace window (default 5s), then PID/start-stamp revalidation, then SIGKILL — the revalidation means a recycled PID is never the kill target (daemon/takeover.go:16-33). A `ResourceOwner` is never killed for being older: the takeover defers unless external proof confirms it dead, and the verdict set is `ExitSelf` (incumbent same-or-newer), `Bind` (socket clear), or `Defer` (daemon/takeover.go:31-57). On the incumbent side, `SkewWatch` compares the running version against the installed artifact and fires only after the configured number of consecutive confirmations, storm-gated by a strike budget — one flapping install never triggers a restart loop (daemon/skew.go:26-33). Idle exit defaults to 2 hours and is vetoed by live attachments; a dead attachment (probed by identity) stops vetoing (daemon/idle.go:14-20).

## Drain-on-upgrade (drain)

The drain engine hands a draining daemon's resources to a strictly newer successor with every row in exactly one journal, defending against crashes, power loss, and concurrent cooperating processes — a hostile same-UID actor is explicitly out of scope (drain/resources.go:1-8). The transition runs a normative, durably recorded a..h order: set the drain flag, close intake, settle admitted work, snapshot the generation journal, truncate canonical ownership, bind the successor's drain listener, release the canonical flock, spawn the successor (drain/transition.go:126-146). Every step callback can replay — a crash between a step's effect and its durable phase record re-runs it — so all of them must be idempotent, and a retried transition must reuse the same generation (drain/transition.go:149-157).

Liveness is tri-state and Undetermined is never Dead: a probe timeout, enumeration failure, or unreadable identity means "do nothing" for every force/reap/adopt gate, and force-clearing requires `ForcePolicyConfirmedDead` — the zero-value policy never forces (drain/liveness.go:12-49). Adoption is equally conservative: the canonical daemon's scan loop adopts a generation's rows only once the owner is proven dead by a complete scan, admitted through the intake gate so it can never race a concurrent drain (drain/scan.go:18-27). Each generation journal has exactly one owner — a reused or foreign generation name never double-owns a key (`ErrStaleJournal`, `ErrStaleGeneration`) — and the drain-root `.lock` is never unlinked; generation names may not collide with it (drain/journal.go:20-38). Fences are held from busy-check through yield and handoff: the sweep re-reads canonical under the fence, `Yield` runs at least once and must be idempotent, and a fence that cannot release must panic, since a stuck fence leaves the resource permanently unretryable (drain/resources.go:29-61).

Request daemons that own nothing external use `drain.Simple` instead: close intake, deactivate the listener, settle admitted work to terminal responses, mark pools closing, cancel executors — graceful order, no journal (drain/simple.go:13-50).

## In-process supervision (supervise)

The supervisor's safety contract is fail-closed. A staleness signal only NOMINATES death; an authoritative `Alive` probe must corroborate before any action, and every probe error reads as not-confirmed-dead — an ambiguous signal never kills or respawns a possibly-live child (supervise/supervise.go:10-16). A failed roster enumeration aborts the tick; it is never "all subjects gone" (supervise/supervise.go:71-74). Kill and respawn run under a per-subject lock shared with the consumer's manual-kill path — back it with a `KeyedMutex` so the same subject key always yields the same mutex — and the subject's status is re-read under that lock immediately before acting (supervise/keyedmutex.go:5-9, supervise/supervise.go:37-42). Respawns count against a sliding-window strike budget (`Limit` within `Window`); an exhausted subject is parked, and `StartupGrace` suppresses nomination for a freshly spawned child (supervise/supervise.go:76-90).

## The launchd matrix (service)

`service.Agent` installs a consumer daemon as a per-user LaunchAgent — never a root daemon, so it can reach the login Keychain — with a fixed plist shape: `RunAtLoad` true, `KeepAlive` true as a plain BOOLEAN (no conditional dict shapes), `ThrottleInterval` 10, `ProcessType` Background (service/service.go:26-55). The lifecycle choreography is bootout, bootstrap, enable, kickstart, with brew-managed installs detected and reconciled (service/service.go:1-7).

`service.AppKeepAlive` keeps a long-lived .app bundle running for the login session with `Program` `/usr/bin/open` and args `-g -W <app>`: `-W` makes `open` block until the app exits AND attach to an already-running instance, so launchd's KeepAlive relaunches only on a real exit and never spins against a live holder; `-g` keeps launches in the background. `AssociatedBundleIdentifiers` is added when a bundle id is set. Unregistering the agent kills the `open` waiter, not the app (service/keepalive.go:15-35). A private, cask-less holder binary is not a bundle `open` can adopt; it stays CLI-spawned via `proc.Spawn` with no LaunchAgent (service/keepalive.go:21-23).

There is no socket-activation surface in daemonkit. And the KeepAlive boolean has a known consumer pattern: a daemon whose clean takeover exit must STAY exited rewrites `KeepAlive` to `{SuccessfulExit: false}` post-render — claude-pool does exactly this — because a bare `true` relaunches even after a deliberate exit-self.

## Stay-active and background-item mechanics

Recorded explicitly: no macOS 26 background-item disclosure API appears anywhere in this code. The stay-active surface is built from the existing mechanisms, and anyone hunting for a dedicated "disclosure" call will not find one. What actually keeps a daemon resident and approved:

- **SMAppService agent registration** — `SMAppService.agent(plistName:)` behind the `LoginItemService` seam, reconciled toward enabled on every launch (not a one-shot flag): enabled is `active`, `requiresApproval` opens the System Settings Login Items pane and returns `pendingApproval`, unregistered registers (Sources/DaemonKit/LoginItem.swift:40-80).
- **`ProcessType` Background** in the Agent plist and **`LSUIElement`** for agent apps — no Dock icon, no menu bar.
- **`AssociatedBundleIdentifiers`** so launchd attributes the agent to the app in Login Items.
- **Stable bundle identity** — macOS keys TCC, notification, and Full Disk Access grants to a bundle's identifier, signing team, and install path; changing ANY of them resets the user's grants, so consumers freeze all three across releases (bundle/bundle.go:1-7).
- **App Group containers only via NSFileManager** — prompt-free access to a group container is contingent on resolving it with `containerURLForSecurityApplicationGroupIdentifier:`; a hand-built `~/Library/Group Containers` join forfeits that contract and triggers a consent prompt, so `appgroup` never constructs the path and fails loud with `ErrNoGroupContainer` (appgroup/appgroup.go:1-8).
- **A fixed state-dir layout** — `paths.Paths` roots everything at `~/<App>` (`daemon.sock`, `daemon.log`, `http.json`, `locks/start.lock`, `subjects/<id>/`), never relocated by an environment variable, so state stays stable regardless of `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` (paths/paths.go:1-6).

## Reference implementation A — hookd (captain-hook, Python)

captain-hook's resident daemon is the Python translation of the spawn half of the contract, with the trust and drain halves deliberately simplified. It is opt-in — nothing changes until a wired hook command points at it — and per-project: worker identity is the real project root plus an additive env subset (`WORKER_ENV_KEYS` plus every `HOOKS_*` var), with per-session vars excluded so one warm worker serves a project across sessions (captain-hook docs/guide/daemon.qmd:8-27, capt_hook_client/key.py:1-14).

The thin client connects first and spawns on miss: a 0700-owned, non-symlinked run dir is verified before any connect (defense in depth — the authority is the per-connection peer-UID check, which closes the TOCTOU), a per-worker flock elects one spawner while losers poll the socket, the winner reclaims a stale socket by unlinking it, and the spawn is detached the same way daemonkit's is — `start_new_session=True`, `close_fds=True`, `stdin=DEVNULL`, 0600 `O_NOFOLLOW` append log (capt_hook_client/client.py:211-338). Trust is the same-user boundary ONLY: kernel peer credentials (`SO_PEERCRED` or `getpeereid`), no codesign requirement (capt_hook_client/client.py:256-263).

Drain is graceful, not durable: shutdown unlinks the socket FIRST so no new connect can land, then serves everything already accepted into the backlog before closing — no journal, no fences, no generation handoff (captain_hook/daemon/server.py:173-191). Upgrade detection is a build-fingerprint watchdog that restarts only after two consecutive mismatches (2s ticks, `DEBOUNCE_TICKS = 2`), and idle exit is the same 2 hours as daemonkit's default (captain_hook/daemon/lifecycle.py:29-74).

## Reference implementation B — Captain Hook desktop helper (Swift)

The desktop helper is the Swift-side consumer of DaemonKit proper. It serves a `SocketServer` at `~/.capt-hook/helper.sock` with 64 KiB frames, a 5s read timeout, and `PeerTrust()` — the UID floor alone, no designated requirement (captain-hook helper/Sources/App/CaptainHookApp.swift:73-78). The protocol is captain-hook's own frozen v1 newline-JSON, hand-serialized in frozen key order and pinned byte-for-byte by a golden fixture (`tests/fixtures/helper-sock-v1.golden.jsonl`) (helper/Sources/Shared/HelperProtocol.swift:1-20).

Residency is SMAppService reconcile-on-launch through DaemonKit's `LoginItem`, an `LSUIElement` agent app, and a LaunchAgent plist that carries `BundleProgram`, `RunAtLoad`, and `AssociatedBundleIdentifiers` — and no `KeepAlive` key at all, so a helper that exits stays exited until relaunched (helper/LaunchAgents/com.yasyf.capt-hook.helper.plist). Clients close that gap themselves: connect first, and on a miss `open -g -a` the app and poll the socket until it answers (captain_hook/helper/client.py:159-201). Drain matches `SocketServer`'s contract: the listener stops accepting and in-flight handlers finish before the socket path is unlinked — graceful, like hookd, not journaled (Sources/DaemonKit/SocketServer.swift:35-40).

## What the reference impls teach

The two non-Go implementations keep the same spine — detached spawn with a flock-elected single entrant, kernel peer credentials as the trust floor, connect-first clients that spawn or launch on miss, and an ordered graceful drain. What they drop is exactly what their risk profile permits: neither owns external resources, so neither carries the durable journal/fence machinery, and neither is a takeover target, so neither needs version-ordered eviction. Port that spine when writing a new daemon in any language; reach for the full drain and takeover engines only when the daemon holds resources a successor must inherit.
ops.when: when designing, migrating, or reviewing a daemon (Go, Swift, or non-Go) that spawns detached, owns a socket single-entrant, does version-aware takeover, or drains on upgrade; or when wiring launchd/SMAppService for one
